qa_pairs.question: How can users request full H100 GPUs for their jobs on the Nibi cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: To request full H100 GPUs on the Nibi cluster, users can utilize specific Slurm options. For one H100-80gb GPU, use `--gpus=h100:1` or `--gpus=h100_80gb:1`. To request multiple H100-80gb GPUs per node, options include `--gpus-per-node=h100:2`, `--gpus-per-node=h100:3`, or `--gpus-per-node=h100:4`. For multiple full H100 GPUs spread across the cluster, use `--gpus=h100:n`, replacing 'n' with the desired total number of GPUs.

qa_pairs.question: What Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) configurations are available for H100 GPUs on the Nibi cluster, and what are the Slurm options to request a single instance?
qa_pairs.answer: Approximately half of the GPU nodes on the Nibi cluster are configured with Multi-Instance GPU (MIG) technology, offering three instance sizes: H100-1g.10gb (1/8th computing power with 10GB GPU memory), H100-2g.20gb (2/8th computing power with 20GB GPU memory), and H100-3g.40gb (3/8th computing power with 40GB GPU memory). To request one of these specific GPU instances for a compute job, use the corresponding Slurm option: `--gpus=h100_1g.10gb:1`, `--gpus=h100_2g.20gb:1`, or `--gpus=h100_3g.40gb:1` respectively.

qa_pairs.question: How can users access the Nibi cluster through a web browser and what features does this access method provide?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can access the Nibi cluster conveniently through a web browser using Open OnDemand (OOD) via the portal at https://ondemand.sharcnet.ca/. After signing in with Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), a user-friendly interface is presented, offering options to open a Bash shell terminal or launch a remote desktop session. This platform also supports running JupyterLab interactively.
